comment.body:
I’m not sure that Putin will let become this war ‘frozen’, he knows that if there will be a ‘pause’, the EU that backs Ukraine will have time to regroup ( they are increasing military spending plan….. I don’t think that putin will let them do this ). One crucial point is that Ukraine  will not give up on recognising Crimea as it is written in their military doctrine. On the contract putin will never grant that land. So trump is mediating and investors appreciate this, especially the good relationship with putin as we’ve seen Friday. As long as a peace will be dealt, Europe should let putin take what he asks, 1997 agreement…..) otherwise war can continue with no tactical interruption of the confit that EU will benefit, Moreover, it seems unlikely that European armies could be deployed, since Putin would see this as a threat—the very threat he is fighting against. 
So here someone has to do a stepback(Russia is winning so more negotiation power). I’m curious to see how things will evolve. So far I don’t see any term in the deal that could get the parties to agree. Russia can be pressured with sanctions, but Ukraine would have to give up some of its demands, or would require more war to achieve.

comment.body: No matter whether there is peace, war or anything in between - the militarization of the EU will go forward. It's completely unrelated. However, the longer the war is in an active state, the longer russia will be weakened no matter whether winning or loosing. So if you think there will be a eu-russia conflict, Putins best option would be as quick out of it as possible, as he is the one who needs to "regroup".

Best way out would be giving up Crimea (people there want to be Russian anyways) and no NATO for Ukraine but protection Status like Israel with the US. Donbass and Kherson would remain in the limbo as it was after the first war. 

However, i am not sure if Ukraine can pull this off. Selensky would need broad support for changing the constitution in regards of Crimea...

comment.body:
If investors are currently skeptical about the US and shifting focus to BRICS, and tariffs are going to bring both inflation and economic slowdown, Powell can't risk an early QE without fueling even more inflation and pushing investors even further away.
This basically means a recession is coming, and it's likely to be a hard one rather than a soft landing, which would scare investors even more.
At that point, even if a peace or trade deal is reached, it would just be a patch over a recession that's already on its way once the effects of the tariffs start hitting? Or could the new trade deals end up giving a strong enough boost to the economy to make the impact of the tariffs less relevant? (Correct me if I misunderstood any of this)

comment.body:
Waller and the FED are looking for… drum roll… massive drop in the labor market in order to step in. On a per hourly basis, it has already been getting hammered.

https://youtu.be/vGnWP_x7Rzs?si=UnGt37AJn-Kre7TA

Joseph Wang in this video wrote a book called “Central Banking 101”. I got the audio book… bad mistake 😂. I’m gonna grab a physical copy as well and jump back into it.

He ironically covers many of Alma’s points, and mentions more, including:

1. Fed needing weak employment numbers to fully bail out.
2. The lows are not likely in
3. Container shipments (research done by Craig Fuller who studies logistics) are declining significantly.
4. There will be a delayed supply shock on store shelves signifying an economic impact beginning in June or July.
5. On tariffs for countries excluding China, companies are splitting the levies into thirds between the company, the importer, and the consumer.
6. Large companies have some margin to burn on absorbing levies. That means, LOWER JUSTIFIABLE PE RATIOS MOVING FORWARD.
7. Bigger companies (presumably retailers like Amazon/Walmart) are going to sacrifice profits short term to capitalize on gaining market share against companies who have to pass levies on to consumers. This should lead to very buyable dips.
8. Tariffs will cause a 1% increase in home prices. Compound that with a weakening economy and the FED’s need for lower employment numbers, I might need to re-short KBH 😂 
9. Less travel and discretionary spending. Probably not good for restaurants either.

comment.body:
Do we have any guess as to what that deal would like? Has Trump secured enough domestic manufacturing agreements to justify canceling these tariffs? Will this further delay the FED cutting rates? Likely leading to our next issue… a potential bond crises?

If employment numbers don’t decline, the FED will view these tariffs as transitory and will dilly dally.

If Bessent and Trump want lower rates and more liquidity, they will likely have to see the job through.

Are they talking to these Russian and ME investors about buying up US debt? Because that’s what they really need right now more than them buying our stocks. Is that part of the deal?

comment.body: The trump tweet posted above could imply that the notion of "tariffs will be eased" is wrong and could start another leg down. But I wouldn't be surprised if we start seeing things like "this is a one time thing...tarriff inflation is transitory" yaddah yaddah as we move down. So I think, retest of a low here into May OPEX but then itll get really choppy until the Fed meeting
